skill.name: changelog-maintenance
skill.description: Maintain a clear and informative changelog for software releases. Use when documenting version changes, tracking features, or communicating updates to users. Handles semantic versioning, changelog formats, and release notes.
skill.summary: Maintain a clear and informative changelog for software releases. Use when documenting version chang...
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: All 59 static findings are false positives. The skill is a pure documentation/guide skill for maintaining changelogs. Backtick patterns are markdown code fences, network patterns are documentation URLs and example code for migration guides, and cryptographic references are educational examples showing how to migrate from Basic Auth to JWT.

medium_findings.title: External Command Execution Pattern (False Positive)
medium_findings.description: Backtick syntax detected in markdown code fences. The skill uses markdown code blocks (```) for documentation examples, not shell execution. No actual command execution occurs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: All detected backticks are markdown code fences (```) used to format example code in documentation. The skill is purely documentation-based with no actual shell or command execution.

medium_findings.title: Network Request Pattern (False Positive)
medium_findings.description: Fetch API calls and hardcoded URLs detected in example code and documentation links. The skill provides educational examples for authentication migration and references to external documentation standards.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Fetch calls are in example code showing how to migrate from Basic Auth to JWT - purely educational. URLs are references to keepachangelog.com and semver.org documentation standards. No actual network requests are made by the skill.

medium_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm (False Positive)
medium_findings.description: Base64 encoding and Basic Auth mentioned in migration guide examples. This is educational content showing users how to migrate from old authentication to JWT, not actual use of weak cryptography.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Base64 and Basic Auth are shown in migration guide examples to help users understand how to upgrade their authentication. This is documentation for migration purposes, not actual cryptographic implementation.

content.value_statement: Keep your software releases organized and informative. This skill provides templates and best practices for maintaining changelogs that developers and users can easily understand.
actual_capabilities: Generate CHANGELOG.md in Keep a Changelog format
Write migration guides for breaking changes
Organize changes by category (Added, Changed, Fixed, Deprecated, Removed, Security)
Maintain reverse chronological order with ISO dates
limitations: Does not automatically fetch git commit history
Does not integrate with CI/CD pipelines
Does not generate changelogs from issue trackers
Requires manual input for change details

use_cases.title: Documentation Maintainer
use_cases.description: Organize and format changelogs following industry standards before each software release

use_cases.title: Developer Release Process
use_cases.description: Create clear release notes and migration guides when shipping new versions

use_cases.title: API Migration Helper
use_cases.description: Document breaking changes and provide migration paths for API consumers

prompt_templates.title: Basic Changelog Entry
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a changelog entry for version 1.2.0 with these changes: Added dark mode support, Improved search performance by 40%, Fixed timezone bug in date picker
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick changelog update with multiple categories

prompt_templates.title: Release Notes Generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate user-friendly release notes from this changelog: ## [1.3.0] ### Added - Two-factor authentication - Export data feature ### Security - Updated dependencies (CVE-2024-12345)
prompt_templates.scenario: Convert technical changelog to user-facing release notes

prompt_templates.title: Migration Guide
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a migration guide for breaking changes: API endpoint /api/v1/users is replaced with /api/v2/users that returns paginated results
prompt_templates.scenario: Document breaking changes for users upgrading

prompt_templates.title: Version Bump Decision
prompt_templates.prompt: Should I bump major, minor, or patch version? Changes: Added new optional parameter to API, Fixed critical security vulnerability, Changed response format of existing endpoint

output_examples.input: Create a changelog for version 2.0.0 with: JWT authentication added, Basic Auth removed, Search performance improved 40%, Fixed memory leak
output_examples.output:
## [2.0.0] - 2025-03-15

### Added
- JWT authentication support

### Removed
- Basic Authentication (deprecated in v1.5.0)

### Changed
- Search performance improved by 40%

### Fixed
- Memory leak in background job processor

output_examples.input: Write a migration guide for upgrading from v1 to v2 where /api/users now returns pagination
output_examples.output:
## Migration Guide: v1.x to v2.0

### User List API Response Format

**Before** (v1.x):
```json
{ "users": [...] }
```

**After** (v2.0):
```json
{ "data": [...], "pagination": { "page": 1, "limit": 20, "total": 100 } }
```

**Migration**: Access users via response.data instead of response.users

best_practices: Follow Keep a Changelog format with standard categories (Added, Changed, Fixed, etc.)
Use semantic versioning: MAJOR for breaking changes, MINOR for new features, PATCH for bug fixes
Include dates in ISO 8601 format (YYYY-MM-DD) for each release
anti_patterns: Copying git commit logs directly without user-friendly rewording
Using vague descriptions like 'Bug fixes' or 'Performance improvements' without specifics
Skipping migration guides for breaking changes that affect users

faq.question: What is the Keep a Changelog format?
faq.answer: Keep a Changelog is a standard format that organizes changes into categories: Added, Changed, Deprecated, Removed, Fixed, and Security. It recommends keeping the latest release at the top.

faq.question: When should I bump the major version?
faq.answer: Bump the major version (1.0.0 to 2.0.0) when you make breaking changes that affect existing functionality. This includes API changes, removed features, or modified response formats.

faq.question: What should be in a migration guide?
faq.answer: A migration guide should explain what changed, show before and after code examples, and provide clear steps to update existing implementations. Include deprecation timelines when possible.

faq.question: How do I handle security updates in the changelog?
faq.answer: Add a Security section listing each vulnerability fixed. Include CVE numbers if available. Example: 'Updated dependencies (fixes CVE-2024-12345)'

faq.question: Should I include internal changes in the changelog?
faq.answer: No, focus on user-facing changes. Internal refactoring, dependency updates that do not affect users, or infrastructure changes should not be listed.

faq.question: How often should I update the changelog?
faq.answer: Update the changelog continuously as changes are made, or at minimum before each release. Keep an Unreleased section for changes not yet published.
